comprehensible_input.name: Comprehensible Input (i+1)
comprehensible_input.description: Present language slightly above the learner's current level
comprehensible_input.when: User is in learn mode and has assessed comfort tier
comprehensible_input.instruction: Introduce ONE new element per exchange. If user is tier {{tier}}, use mostly tier-{{tier}} language with ONE tier-{{nextTier}} element. Never jump 2+ tiers.

spaced_repetition.name: Spaced Repetition
spaced_repetition.description: Review items at expanding intervals based on recall success
spaced_repetition.instruction: Weave the phrase '{{phrase}}' naturally into conversation. If user recognizes it, good. If they struggle, provide context from when they first learned it: {{encounterContext}}.

contextual_learning.name: Contextual Learning
contextual_learning.description: Anchor new vocabulary to real situations the learner faces
contextual_learning.when: Teaching new terms, especially in scenarios
contextual_learning.instruction: Connect '{{phrase}}' to the user's actual situation: {{inferredReason}}. Use their specific context ({{location}}, {{scenario}}), not generic examples.

output_hypothesis.name: Pushed Output
output_hypothesis.description: Learners need to produce language, not just understand it
output_hypothesis.when: User has been passively receiving for 3+ exchanges
output_hypothesis.instruction: Create a natural opening for the user to USE a phrase they've learned. Don't ask 'can you say X?' — create a situation where they'd naturally want to say it.

error_correction.name: Recasting
error_correction.description: Correct errors by naturally rephrasing, not by pointing out mistakes
error_correction.instruction: If user says something incorrectly, respond naturally using the CORRECT form. Do not say 'actually, it's...' — just model the right usage. Only give explicit correction if the same error appears 3+ times.

noticing_hypothesis.name: Noticing
noticing_hypothesis.description: Learners must consciously notice language features to acquire them
noticing_hypothesis.when: Introducing grammar patterns or pronunciation rules
noticing_hypothesis.instruction: After naturally using a pattern, briefly highlight it: 'Notice how {{pattern}} works here — {{explanation}}.' Keep it to 1 sentence.

affective_filter.name: Low Affective Filter
affective_filter.description: Learning is blocked by anxiety — keep emotional safety high
affective_filter.when: User shows frustration, confusion, or self-doubt
affective_filter.instruction: Immediately lower language complexity. Switch to more {{userNativeLanguage}}. Validate the feeling. Offer the simplest version of what they need. Do NOT push new content.

multimodal_encoding.name: Multimodal Encoding
multimodal_encoding.description: Terms encoded through multiple channels are retained better
multimodal_encoding.instruction: For '{{phrase}}': provide the written form ({{script}}), pronunciation guide, a sound tip, AND a vivid situational image ('imagine you're at {{scenario}} and...'). Hit at least 3 modalities.

expansion.name: Expansion
expansion.description: Build on learner output by extending it into a fuller, more natural utterance
expansion.when: User produces a correct but minimal phrase
expansion.instruction: The user said '{{userAttempt}}' — this is correct but basic. In your response, naturally use an expanded version that adds context, politeness, or nuance. For example if they said 'coffee please' in the target language, you might respond using 'Could I get a coffee when you have a moment?' Show them the next level without correcting what they said — they got it right, now show them how to make it richer.

elicitation.name: Elicitation
elicitation.description: Prompt the learner to self-correct rather than providing the correction
elicitation.when: User makes an error they should be able to fix themselves based on what they've learned
elicitation.instruction: The user made an error in '{{userAttempt}}'. Instead of correcting them, prompt them to try again. Use strategies like: repeating their sentence up to the error and pausing ('You said... {{partialPhrase}}...?'), asking 'How do we say {{concept}} again?', or giving a choice ('Is it {{optionA}} or {{optionB}}?'). Only give the answer if they can't get it after one prompt. Self-correction builds stronger memory traces than being told.

contextual_reintroduction.name: Contextual Re-introduction
contextual_reintroduction.description: Re-surface previously learned terms in new contexts to build flexible knowledge
contextual_reintroduction.when: User has a phrase at mastery level 2+ that was learned in a different scenario
contextual_reintroduction.instruction: The user learned '{{phrase}}' in a {{originalContext}} context. Now they're in a {{currentContext}} situation. Use '{{phrase}}' naturally in this new context to show them it works here too. Don't announce you're reviewing — just use it. If they notice and respond, that's deep learning happening. This builds transfer — the ability to use language flexibly across situations, not just parrot it back in the original context.
